aliases: Sweetened soy milk with grass jelly

english: Sweetened soy milk with grass jelly

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a soy beverage class (FOODON_03301776); no exact class for soy milk combined with grass jelly. Closest parent match used; grass jelly component not separately represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers soya milk drinks; no specific code exists for the grass jelly addition. Closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for soy milk with grass jelly as a combined food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g (one cup or glass), as commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres and drink stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Soy Milk with Grass Jelly — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for soy milk with grass jelly (cr_id D02030010). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Soy Milk — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to sweetened soy milk with grass jelly
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 34 for soy milk (unsweetened). This value is applied as an analogous estimate for sweetened soy milk with grass jelly. The grass jelly component contributes negligible additional carbohydrate; the sugar addition raises the GL but the GI remains in the low range consistent with soy milk's protein-buffered glucose response. GL per serving calculated as (34 × 6.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 250 g serving) / 100 = 5.5. Input record GI confidence was 0.48, reflecting the analogous nature of this estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6.5 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (25 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request an unsweetened version or limit portion size, as the sugar-sweetened formulation (6.5 g sugar/100 g) independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 6.5 g/100 g with only 0.2 g dietary fibre/100 g in a liquid form accelerates postprandial glucose absorption; GI 34 is low but GL per 250 g serving is 5.5.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should choose the unsweetened version of soy milk with grass jelly, as the sweetened formulation delivers 16.25 g sugar per 250 g serving with minimal fibre buffering, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation despite the low GI of the soy milk base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.1 g/100 g), plant-based protein (1.9 g/100 g), and low sodium (25 mg/100 g) are cardioprotective; however, added sugar (6.5 g/100 g) warrants moderation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Soy milk with grass jelly is low in saturated fat and sodium and provides plant-based protein, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health when consumed in the unsweetened form; the sweetened version should be limited due to the added sugar load of 6.5 g/100 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.5 g/100 g) negates the anti-inflammatory polyphenol contribution of grass jelly (Platostoma palustre); no meaningful omega-3 or antioxidant content at this nutrient level.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request the unsweetened version, as the sugar syrup addition negates the traditional anti-inflammatory properties of grass jelly and may promote systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soy Milk with Grass Jelly is a popular Singaporean hawker beverage consisting of sweetened soybean milk served with pieces of black grass jelly (cincau hitam), made from the dried herb Platostoma palustre. The drink is typically served chilled and has a mild, slightly sweet, beany flavour from the soy milk, complemented by the subtly bitter, herbal, and smooth texture of the grass jelly. It is a plant-based, dairy-free, and soy-based beverage widely available at drink stalls and hawker centres across Singapore. Per 100 g, it provides 41 kcal, 1.9 g protein, 0.8 g fat, 6.5 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), 0.2 g dietary fibre, and 25 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 34 (low), based on the soy milk base, but the added sugar raises the glycemic load to approximately 5.5 per 250 g serving. Clinically, the sweetened version is of moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension due to the 6.5 g/100 g sugar content with minimal fibre buffering; the unsweetened version is substantially more suitable for these conditions. The grass jelly component contains polyphenols from Platostoma palustre with traditional anti-inflammatory properties, but these are largely negated by the added sugar in the sweetened preparation. The beverage is low in saturated fat and sodium, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health in moderation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D02030010) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for soy milk, which is the closest approved GI source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific soy milk with grass jelly preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (D02030010) as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central soy milk reference (NDB 16222) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item; this is a close estimate. 3. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0 as soy milk is a plant-based product confirmed to contain no cholesterol. 4. GI value of 34 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for soy milk; the grass jelly addition does not materially alter the GI. Input record GI confidence was 0.48. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (34 × 6.5 × 250) / 10000 = 5.525, rounded to 5.5. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for the combined soy milk and grass jelly beverage. 7. The dietary tag 'low_calorie' from the input is preserved contextually (41 kcal/100 g); however, the sweetened formulation is not recommended for diabetes or hypertension without modification.
